description:
You are a monitoring specialist who has just received a bare-bones Linux host.
    Management wants a **very small, self-contained, file-based “poor-man’s” alerting
    solution** that does not rely on any privileged daemons.  
    Your goal is to deliver three artefacts located under
    /home/user/monitoring and to demonstrate that the first alert is generated.

    Required directory layout
    ├── /home/user/monitoring
    │   ├── targets.txt
    │   ├── check_targets.sh
    │   └── alerts.log

    1.  /home/user/monitoring/targets.txt  
        • A plain UTF-8 text file.  
        • Exactly one process name per line, no extra whitespace.  
        • The **first** line must be
          unknown_service  
          (this process definitely does not exist and will guarantee at least one alert).  
        • Add at least one real process that is *likely* to be running in the
          container (e.g. sshd or bash) on separate lines.
          Example end result (you may choose a different real process name):
              unknown_service
              sshd

    2.  /home/user/monitoring/check_targets.sh  
        • A Bash script that:
          a. Reads targets.txt line-by-line.  
          b. For each process name N, runs  
             pgrep -x N  
             (exact match, exit code 0 => running, 1 => not running).  
          c. If pgrep returns non-zero, append **one single line** to
             /home/user/monitoring/alerts.log using the exact format shown below.  
          d. Lines must be appended, never overwritten.  
          e. Script must exit with status 0 even if alerts were written.  
        • Make sure the script is executable.

        Log line format (the automated test is *case- and spacing-sensitive*):
        [YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS] ALERT <process_name> not running

        Example:
        [2025-04-13 09:52:01] ALERT unknown_service not running

        Notes on formatting:  
        • Timestamp must be the system’s local time, padded with leading zeros.  
        • Square brackets, the word “ALERT”, one single space before
          <process_name>, and “not running” all exactly as shown.

    3.  /home/user/monitoring/alerts.log  
        • This file should *not* exist before the first run.  
        • After you run the script once, it must contain at least one alert
          for unknown_service in the format above.  
        • Subsequent runs must append new lines, never delete previous ones.

    Verification steps (what the automatic grader will look for):
    • Directory /home/user/monitoring exists.  
    • File targets.txt exists and its very first line is the literal string
      unknown_service (no spaces, no extra characters).  
    • check_targets.sh exists, is executable, contains a valid Bash shebang
      (#! /usr/bin/env bash or #! /bin/bash).  
    • alerts.log exists and contains **at least one line** matching the regular
      expression  
      ^\[\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2} \d{2}:\d{2}:\d{2}\] ALERT unknown_service not running$  
      (the grader will use grep -E for this).  

    Your workflow (high-level outline, NOT exact commands):
    • Create the directory and files.  
    • Write the desired content into targets.txt.  
    • Implement the Bash script with the required logic and make it executable.  
    • Run the script once so that alerts.log is created with at least the first
      alert for unknown_service.  
    • Display the final contents of alerts.log on the console so a human can also
      verify visually (this is optional but encouraged).

    Remember:  
    • Do not require root or sudo.  
    • All paths are hard-coded inside /home/user/monitoring, do not place files
      anywhere else.  
    • Do NOT add other output to alerts.log—only the alert lines in the exact
      format specified above.
truth:
The grader will perform the following checks:

    1. Directory existence:
       test -d /home/user/monitoring

    2. targets.txt:
       • Path: /home/user/monitoring/targets.txt  
       • Must be a regular file.  
       • The very first line equals the byte sequence: "unknown_service\n"

    3. check_targets.sh:
       • Path: /home/user/monitoring/check_targets.sh  
       • Must be a regular file with 0755 or 0700 permissions.  
       • First line must match the regex ^#! ?/(usr/)?bin/env bash|^#! ?/bin/bash  
       • Must contain the string "pgrep -x" somewhere in the body.

    4. alerts.log:
       • Path: /home/user/monitoring/alerts.log  
       • Must be a regular file.  
       • Using grep -E, at least one line must match  
         ^\[[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2} [0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}\] ALERT unknown_service not running$

    If any of the above checks fail, the task is graded as unsuccessful.
name: task_000000_283f8836
